skill.name: ddd-tactical-patterns
skill.description: Apply DDD tactical patterns in code using entities, value objects, aggregates, repositories, and domain events with explicit invariants.
skill.summary: Model behavior-rich domain code with DDD tactical patterns and clear aggregate boundaries.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: AI review found the static detections to be false positives caused by DDD terminology and markdown examples. No shell execution, weak cryptography, reconnaissance behavior, network access, data exfiltration, or prompt injection text was found in the reviewed files. The skill is instructional content for tactical Domain-Driven Design patterns and is safe to publish.

low_findings.title: False Positive: External Command Detection
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged markdown backticks and a reference-file instruction as Ruby or shell execution. The reviewed lines contain a markdown file reference and a TypeScript example, with no executable shell command or runtime invocation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is markdown prose or a fenced TypeScript class example. No subprocess call, shell syntax, or user-controlled command path is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Detection
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged DDD guidance lines as weak cryptography. The reviewed lines discuss descriptions, aggregate design, and invariant modeling; they do not mention or implement cryptographic algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain no crypto API names, hash algorithms, cipher modes, keys, or encoded payloads. They are plain Domain-Driven Design documentation.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Detection
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged DDD checklist and example lines as system reconnaissance. The reviewed lines describe aggregate consistency, ORM isolation, and order submission logic, not host inspection or environment discovery.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are design guidance and a method signature. No commands, filesystem enumeration, process inspection, or network discovery behavior appears.

content.value_statement: Domain code often spreads rules across services, controllers, and persistence models. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code shape entities, value objects, aggregates, repositories, and domain events around explicit invariants.
actual_capabilities: Identifies domain invariants before proposing aggregate boundaries.
Guides immutable value object modeling with validation at construction.
Moves business behavior from controllers into domain objects.
Defines repository contracts around aggregate roots.
Suggests domain events for meaningful state transitions.
Provides a tactical checklist for aggregate, repository, and event design.
limitations: Does not define strategic bounded contexts or context maps.
Does not choose databases, message brokers, or transport protocols.
Does not replace domain expert review of business rules.
Should be paired with tests that verify invariant behavior.

use_cases.title: Refactor Anemic Domain Models
use_cases.description: Move scattered business rules into entities, value objects, and aggregate methods with explicit invariants.

use_cases.title: Design Aggregate Boundaries
use_cases.description: Evaluate transaction boundaries and decide which objects belong inside each aggregate root.

use_cases.title: Review Domain Event Design
use_cases.description: Check event names, payload stability, and versioning before connecting domain changes to integrations.

prompt_templates.title: Find Domain Invariants
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ddd-tactical-patterns skill to identify the main invariants in these requirements. List the entity, value object, aggregate, and event candidates.

prompt_templates.title: Model a Value Object
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ddd-tactical-patterns skill to design a value object for this concept. Include validation rules, equality behavior, and invalid examples.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor Domain Behavior
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ddd-tactical-patterns skill to refactor this service-heavy code into behavior-rich domain objects. Preserve behavior and explain the aggregate boundaries.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Aggregate and Event Boundaries
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ddd-tactical-patterns skill to review this proposed aggregate model. Check invariants, repository boundaries, domain events, and risks from cross-aggregate consistency.

output_examples.input: Review an order submission workflow for tactical DDD modeling.
output: Aggregate root: Order, because submission changes order state inside one transaction boundary.
Invariant: An order cannot be submitted without at least one item.
Value objects: OrderId, Money, Quantity, and ShippingAddress.
Domain event: OrderSubmitted with stable identifiers and minimal payload fields.

output_examples.input: Improve a service that validates price, quantity, and discount rules before saving an invoice.
output: Move price and quantity validation into value objects.
Place discount rules on the Invoice aggregate when they depend on invoice state.
Keep persistence behind an InvoiceRepository contract.
Add tests for invalid totals, duplicate submission, and discount limits.

output_examples.input: Check whether Customer and Order should be one aggregate.
output: Keep Customer and Order separate unless one transaction must enforce a shared invariant.
Reference Customer by identity from Order instead of embedding the full customer model.
Use a domain event when order changes need customer-related follow-up work.

best_practices: Start with invariants, then choose aggregate boundaries around required consistency.
Keep value objects immutable and validate them when they are created.
Name domain events in past tense and keep payloads minimal and stable.
anti_patterns: Do not place core business rules only in controllers or application services.
Do not expose ORM entities as the public domain model.
Do not merge aggregates only to simplify queries or reporting.

faq.question: What does this skill help me design?
faq.answer: It helps design entities, value objects, aggregates, repositories, and domain events for tactical Domain-Driven Design.

faq.question: Can it define bounded contexts?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on tactical code patterns after strategic boundaries are already understood.

faq.question: Does it generate production-ready code?
faq.answer: It can guide code structure, but the result still needs project-specific implementation, tests, and review.

faq.question: When should I avoid this skill?
faq.answer: Avoid it when the task is only UI layout, API documentation, or a simple CRUD workflow without meaningful domain rules.

faq.question: How should I validate the model it suggests?
faq.answer: Review the invariants with domain experts and add tests for valid transitions, invalid transitions, and edge cases.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use it during refactoring?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides guidance that Claude Code and Codex can apply while reorganizing domain behavior in an existing codebase.
